section: Extreme high accuracy prediction and design of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel using machine learning

context_update.summary: This chunk covers the paper title, abstract, introduction, methodology flowchart (Fig. 1), and Table 1 (CGAN parameters). The study proposes a data-driven model combining ML, FA, and CGANs to predict the solid solution strengthening theory of Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel. SVR performed best. After introducing a modified solid solution strengthening theory, the R² improved from 0.85 to 0.89, and RMSE decreased from 0.39 to 0.31. Three alloys (Sample 1, 2, 3) were fabricated by cladding for experimental validation; their predicted versus experimental microhardness values (521/515 HV error 1.17%, 490/500 HV error 2.00%, 415/424 HV error 2.09%) are shown in Fig. 1. The modified theory formula Δσ_sss = Z·M·G·δ_Ec·D_Ec appears in Fig. 1. Table 1 details the CGAN architecture (generator: noise dim 100, hidden layers 64→128, output 12; discriminator: input 12, hidden 128→64, sigmoid output; L2=0.01, ReLU, batch norm). The introduction discusses ML applications in steel and solid solution strengthening background. Subsequent chunks should remember sample aliases, cladding route, microhardness data, the modified SSS formula, and the ML-CGAN framework.
key_variables: composition (Fe, C, Cr, Mn, Si, Ni, Mo, V, W)
properties: microhardness: Sample 1 predicted 521 HV vs. experimental 515 HV (1.17% error); Sample 2 490 vs. 500 HV (2.00%); Sample 3 415 vs. 424 HV (2.09%)
mechanisms: modified solid solution strengthening: Δσ_sss = Z·M·G·δ_Ec·D_Ec
figures_or_tables: Fig. 1 (flowchart, element importance, predicted vs. experimental microhardness)

context_update.summary: This chunk details the data preparation and feature engineering pipeline for the ML model. Starting from 173 Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si steel data points (11 elements + microhardness), outliers are removed via Z-score (|z|>1.50) and isolation forest (<0 or >0.15), yielding 98 clean points. CGANs augment the data to 192 points. Then 41 features are standardized. Feature selection involves RFE with RF, LightGBM, XGBoost voting; Pearson correlation (threshold ≥0.95) to identify high-correlation pairs; and redundant feature elimination by retaining the feature with higher average importance from ET, DT, RF, XGBoost. Exhaustive screening finalizes the feature combination. Optimization algorithm parameters are provided: FA (500 iter, 100 fireflies, α=0.01, βmin=0.2, γ=1), SA (500 iter, T0=100, cooling 0.95), PSO (500 iter, 100 particles, c1=0.8, c2=0.5, w=0.5), GWO (500 iter, 100 wolves, α=2, β=1.5, δ=0.5, a=2). Cladding parameters are set (current 440-600 A, voltage 30-32 V, speed 24 m/h, interlayer 380-410 °C, wire feed 76.8 m/h). Later chunks will use these data, features, and parameters for model training and inverse design.
processing_route: Z-score and isolation forest outlier removal
Exhaustive screening for final feature set
Cladding: current 440-600 A, voltage 30-32 V, speed 24 m/h, interlayer 380-410 °C, wire feed 76.8 m/h

context_update.summary: Section 2.1.3 describes algorithm selection and optimization for microhardness prediction. Ten ML models (SVR, ET, XGBoost, Ridge, LightGBM, MLP, RF, LR, DT, KNN) were trained, with R² and RMSE as evaluation metrics. Bayesian optimization was used to minimize RMSE for hyperparameter tuning. The original solid solution strengthening theory was modified using selected features. A validation set of 117 MatWeb data points was used with ten-fold cross-validation. Section 2.2 outlines inverse design: composition ranges were defined for C (0.02–2 wt%), Si (0.1–1 wt%), Mn (0.15–2 wt%), Cr (0.5–25 wt%), S (0–0.035 wt%), Ni (0–2 wt%), P (0–0.045 wt%), Mo (0–3 wt%), V (0–0.5 wt%), W (0–2 wt%). FA, SA, PSO, and GWO algorithms were employed with parameters in Table 3. Section 2.3 details coating preparation: arc cladding on ASTM A36 substrates (300 mm × 120 mm × 40 mm) using optimized wire composition; cladding parameters in Table 4. Characterization: XRD with Cu Kα at 40 kV/40 mA; Vickers microhardness with 1 kg load, 20 s holding time. Figures 2–4 provide correlation, feature selection, and exhaustive screening results. Future chunks need to remember the inverse-designed composition ranges, the ten ML models, the validation approach, substrate material, characterization conditions, and the modified solid solution strengthening theory.
processing_route: inverse design using FA, SA, PSO, GWO
key_variables: XRD conditions: Cu Kα, 40 kV, 40 mA
mechanisms: modified solid solution strengthening theory using selected features

context_update.summary: This chunk describes characterization methods for the coatings (XRD with Cu Kα at 40 kV/40 mA; Vickers microhardness with 1 kg load, 20 s holding time) and presents early ML modeling results. Element screening reveals positive correlation of Cr, Ni, Mo with microhardness (Fig. 2). RFE with RF, LightGBM, XGBoost identifies nine significant features (Fig. 3a); after removing highly correlated pairs, e/a, G, E, δ, D_EC remain key (Fig. 3c). Exhaustive search yields optimal 3-feature combination {δ_Ec, D_Ec, G} with minimum RMSE 0.257 (Fig. 4). Mechanistic insights cover e/a ratio thresholds for FCC/BCC phase stability, shear modulus role in resisting plastic deformation, second ionization energy effects on solid solution strengthening, and cohesive energy mismatch parameters describing bonding. Table 5 provides SVR hyperparameters. Subsequent chunks will involve further model optimization and experimental validation; remember the final key features and the exhaustive search outcome.
processing_route: X-ray diffraction with Cu Kα at 40 kV/40 mA
Vickers microhardness at 1 kg load, 20 s holding time
key_variables: features (EI-2nd, ηB, w, δEI-2nd, DEc, δEc, r, G, e/a)
final key features (e/a, G, E, δ, D_EC)
mechanisms: Cr, Ni, Mo form solid solution alloys and carbides; Ni reduces grain size
e/a < 1.65 favors FCC, e/a > 2.05 favors BCC
shear modulus G increases solute atom–dislocation interaction energy
second ionization energy E_I-2nd involves electron cloud interaction
cohesive energy mismatch δ and D_Ec describe bonding states

context_update.summary: This chunk covers Sections 3.1.3–4, concluding the paper. It reports the screening of nine ML models for microhardness prediction using features {G, δ_Ec, D_Ec}; SVR achieved the highest R²=0.85 and lowest RMSE=0.39 (Fig. 5, Table 5). The model was then optimized by integrating a modified solid solution strengthening equation Δσ_sss = Z·M·G·δ_Ec·D_Ec (Z=0.0056, M=3.06), improving R² to 0.89 and RMSE to 0.31 (Fig. 6). For inverse design, the firefly algorithm (FA) yielded the highest microhardness of 733.59 HV (Fig. 7). Three Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si alloy coatings were fabricated by cladding with compositions in Table 6. XRD (Fig. 8) showed Fe-Cr (Sample 1) and Fe-Cr-Ni (Samples 2,3) phases; the diffraction peaks shifted right due to atomic radius differences causing lattice distortion. Microhardness validation (Fig. 9) gave minimum error 1.17% (Sample 1: 521 pred. vs 515 exp.); the solid solution phases were confirmed by δr<0.066 and appropriate ΔHmix. Key conclusions: Cr has the greatest positive effect, and the cohesive energy difference and shear modulus are critical. This chunk provides final validation results, model improvements, and composition-property correlations for the Fe-C-Cr-Mn-Si system.
microstructure: lattice distortion due to atomic radius differences (Cr, Ni vs Fe)
mechanisms: modified solid solution strengthening: Δσ_sss = Z·M·G·δ_Ec·D_Ec
solid solution phase formation criteria: δr<0.066 and -11.6<ΔHmix<3.2 kJ/mol
lattice distortion from atomic radius mismatch
